data.title: Ambev: 2026 World Cup And Brazil's Rate Cuts Create A Powerful Recovery Setup
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Ambev (ABEV), I highlighted their strong financial position, compelling valuation, and attractive dividend despite near-term pressure from broader macro factors, reiterating their Buy rating.

Although the stock is up about 20% since covering it a bit over a month ago and 40% since beginning the coverage back in August, I still believe ABEV has some room left to grow, as the company can benefit from a mix of near- and long-term tailwinds as the region’s leader.

Internal Developments

Ambev’s Q4 report was solid overall, with non-GAAP EPS in line with the market’s expectations and a slight beat on revenue, with solid results across their key markets, delivering a 5.6% increase in Organic Normalized EBITDA in FY25 vs FY24 despite a 3.3% drop in volumes.

The company's free cash flow dropped in FY25 to R$19.86 billion (US$3.80 billion) compared to R$21.35 billion (US$4.09 billion) in 2024, mostly as a result of unfavorable working capital changes, but it's still not terrible for a market cap of ~$48.89 billion, placing them at a P/FCF ratio of 12.87 during a relatively weak year.

Alongside a couple of important near-term tailwinds that I'll mention below, Ambev's guidance expects Brazil Beer Cash COGS (cost of goods sold) per hectoliter to grow between 4.5% and 7.5% in 2026, mostly as a result of commodity headwinds stemming from higher aluminum prices and portfolio mix effects, while the company plans to reinvest into organic growth alongside disciplined M&A activity while returning excess cash to shareholders.

Financially, based on ABEV's latest report, we continue to see a very solid position, with the current assets almost covering their current liabilities and nearly zero debt, with ~US$3.57 billion worth of cash and cash equivalents available, which adds a lot of flexibility to an already strong industry leader, which can potentially use debt in the future if they plan a massive acquisition or aggressive expansion.

Macro Developments

I’ll begin once again with the weakness seen in the US, as that can have significant effects for Ambev as well. Although improving recently thanks to the latest economic data, the situation in the US advanced in an even more uncertain direction, with renewed tariff and geopolitical fears, a weakening dollar, Japan’s economic plans potentially hurting US treasuries, rising national debt leading to crisis worries, and several other factors that came in the brief period since the previous article was published - and this comes at the same time as US consumer confidence crashed to the lowest level since 2014.

All of these can put further pressure not only on the US consumer but also on the market’s expectations, as long-term rates are often a matter of much more than just what the Fed does, with factors such as the country’s growth prospects and risks being taken into account.

Although the situation in Ambev’s key markets isn’t that much better, as this weakness is ultimately a common thing in a high-rate environment, these factors in the US can lead to the market looking for ways to diversify, while the risk of the LATAM region could improve relative to the traditionally risk-free US, which is where Ambev can stand to benefit quite a bit as one of the biggest companies in the region.

However, another very important catalyst for Ambev and its consumers could come from the fact that Brazilian interest rates are expected to be cut significantly this year, with the market now expecting 50 basis point cuts at each of the next 7 policy meetings in 2026 after it was maintained at the latest meeting.

This is expected to bring the country’s Selic rate to 11.5% by the end of 2026 compared to 15% now, which can be an excellent catalyst for Ambev and Brazilian equities in general, especially if the market would be focused on the global diversification mentioned above, while it can also benefit its consumers and economy in general since this would be the opposite of rate hikes, which are meant to slow down the economy to fight inflation.

I’ll also mention once again that 2026 can turn out to be a great year for Ambev and the industry, as the management also expects. Alongside a holiday-rich calendar in Brazil this year, the FIFA World Cup will take place during the summer of 2026, which could be excellent for a significant boost in demand for beer/alcohol, with Ambev’s management even calling it “a great opportunity to connect again 2 of the greatest passions in Latin America, beer and soccer” back during their Q3 Earnings Call.

Regarding premiumization and other health-focused trends such as GLP-1, the company’s CEO highlighted the following during their Q4 Earnings Call, which

There are 2 different kinds of impact. One is attitudinal change. The other one is behavioral change. What happened last year was a change on the behavioral side due to the weather, mostly, okay? (...) And this is what explains the majority of the drop that we saw last year. (...) we are working with a very versatile category to attend more needs and trends. That's the reason why you see us developing zero alcohol beer. We are developing functional beers like gluten-free, lower calories. And we are also attending those consumers with more sweet flavor beers like Flying Fish that we introduced last year. So regarding the point about the GLP-1, we haven't observed any meaningful impact on our business. But like any other emerging trend, it requires time, more evidence, and as a consequence, I just want to say that we're going to keep monitoring and acting accordingly.

Ultimately, I believe it’s important to distinguish between the long-term premiumization trends and the weakness seen in recent years due to the environment. Yes, premiumization is a long-term trend that has been developing for decades as people switch to quality and specific niches over quantity, but the company has all the resources they need to not only adapt to these trends but even be the one that sets them as a global leader.

Risks Reiterated

Still, that doesn’t mean long-term premiumization trends won’t be a threat for the company’s volumes, and even though they can try to counter them with higher prices (which they already do, as premium/certain niches are sold above the average price index), that doesn’t guarantee a positive outcome for Ambev and its industry.

Meanwhile, broader macro pressure can continue alongside these long-term fundamental trends, potentially weakening Ambev’s ability to adapt due to lower budgets, which can have consequences on their results and the market’s expectations, while a disappointing 2026 despite the near-term tailwinds could be even worse for its valuation.

Valuation

For Ambev’s DCF to Equity valuation, I’ll start with their FCF of $3.80 billion in 2025 (not accounting for net borrowings because I want to capture their "true" organic potential), which I’ll continue to assume can grow at a CAGR of 4.5% over the first 5 years thanks to potential improvements in the environment, rate cuts, accretive M&A or investments (which is well below the market’s EPS and even revenue estimates), followed by a CAGR of 3.5% for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, as well as a discount rate of 11% for a solid margin of safety but also to account for their potential, with 15.67 billion shares outstanding as of their Q3 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $51.24 billion, for an intrinsic value of $3.27 per share, which is slightly above the current $3.11 despite using a relatively conservative margin of safety and growth expectations below the market’s estimates.

Conclusion

I reiterate ABEV’s Buy rating, as the company remains in a very solid financial position, is able to produce a lot of cash, and should stand to benefit from near- and long-term tailwinds as a market leader.

There are clearly risks coming from the region and broader environment, but improvements in these can also turn out to be strong opportunities, placing the overall risk-reward still in the company’s favor.

data.title: Biggest S&P 500 Winners Last 5, 10, 20 Years
data.description:
The S&P 500's annualized total return over the last five years has been roughly 13.5%, but the average stock currently in the index has seen an annualized total return about 2.6 percentage points less at just 10.9%.

Just over 20% of stocks in the S&P 500 have posted annualized returns of 20%+ over the last five years, but below is a list of the 15 companies with the strongest 5-year returns. Each of these stocks has gained more than 40% annually since early 2021!

While Nvidia (NVDA) is by far the biggest stock on the list of five-year winners and ranks second, Comfort Systems (FIX) - which installs HVAC and building systems - gets the gold with a 5-year annualized total return of 87.2%.

Super Micro (SMCI) gets the bronze at 56.9%, followed by Howmet Aerospace (HWM) and EMCOR (EME). Other notables on the list of 5-year winners include Broadcom (AVGO), Arista Networks (ANET), Quanta (PWR), Seagate (STX), and General Electric (GE). While GE struggled during the first 20 years of this century, it has lit a fire during the 2020s.

We also looked at returns over the last ten and twenty years to find stocks that have been the most consistent winners over lengthy time frames. As mentioned earlier, the S&P 500 has returned 13.5% annually over the last five years. Over the last ten years, the index has returned 15.6% annually, while it has gained 10.8% annually over the last twenty years.

In the S&P right now, there are only five stocks that have doubled the index annually over the last five, ten, and twenty years. These five stocks are shown below sorted by current market cap.

Three semiconductor stocks make up the list of five (NVDA, Lam Research (LRCX), and KLA (KLAC)), while the other two are the aforementioned Comfort Systems and an energy stock: Texas Pacific Land (TPL).

While there are only five S&P 500 stocks that have doubled the return of the index itself over the last five, ten, and twenty years, there are ten stocks that have posted 20%+ annualized returns over all three time frames.

These ten stocks are highlighted below, once again led by Nvidia, Lam Research, and KLA. Additional stocks that have yet to be mentioned are Amphenol (APH), Monolithic Power (MPWR), and Axon Enterprise (AXON).

So far we have only discussed winners, but we'll close with a list of S&P 500 stocks that have struggled mightily in the last two decades. In the index right now, there are nine stocks that have posted annualized total returns of less than 5% over each of the last five, ten, and twenty years.

Some of the more well-known names on the list include Zimmer (ZBH), Baxter (BAX), Conagra (CAG), and Campbell's (CPB).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Wynn Resorts: Premium Positioning Powers A Multi-Year Growth Story
data.description:
Introduction

Wynn Resorts (WYNN) just reported its Q4 results for the year, and business results were mixed in that there were both positives and negatives. While revenues held steady and softer Vegas and Boston results were offset by momentum in Macau, I think investors are missing the bigger picture. Macau is now driving the largest share of revenues through strong VIP and premium growth, and Wynn's portfolio has some of the best luxury integrated resorts and casinos. In addition, the company has a strong balance sheet that’s allowed for significant capex investment to build out the Wynn Al Marjan Island tower in the UAE, which is on track for a spring 2027 opening and should start to contribute meaningfully to free cash flow.

It’s for those reasons the stock trades expensively on trailing numbers, but I think for investors who can look out a few years to potential EBITDA acceleration and free cash flow growth ahead, they’ll likely be well rewarded.

A Look Back at Q4 and FY'25 Results

Wynn Resorts wrapped up 2025 with a weaker-than-expected Q4. Revenues came in at $1.87 billion, up slightly compared to last year’s $1.84 billion and in line with consensus, but EPS of $1.17 missed by 31 cents and was down compared to last year.

When looking at what drove results this year, Wynn's Las Vegas operations generated $688 million of revenue, 1.6% lower compared to last year. While it continues to be a market leader with share gains and had a table games win percentage that came in at the high end at 26%, it did have a slight dip in RevPAR (revenue per available room) from management deliberately prioritizing higher rates over occupancy.

At Boston Harbor, revenues for the quarter came in at $210.2 million, about 1.1% lower, with slot revenues up 2% with growth in table drop and RevPAR. At Macau, the company continues to invest capex through the recently opened Gourmet Pavilion, Wynn Macau room refresh, and Chairman's Club Expansion at Wynn Palace (tripling its size to nearly 100,000 square feet with premium gaming and bespoke amenities, approved just in time for Chinese New Year). All of these are efforts to capture high-value customers in premium segments across its growing global footprint. Revenues in Macau now make up the company’s largest share at $967.7 million, and growth is being delivered through volume momentum, with VIP turnover up 48%.

When looking at overall metrics and profitability for the year, total property EBITDA now sits at over $2.2 billion a year. Las Vegas achieved $240.8 million in EBITDA at a 35% margin and benefitted from an $8 million positive hold swing despite a 4.1% increase in daily operating expense to $4.6 million from payroll, repairs, and bad debt. Boston generated $57 million in EBITDA at a 27.1% margin, supported by cost controls that limited daily opex growth to under 1% despite labor pressures. Finally, Macau produced $270.9 million in EBITDA at a 28% margin. That was weighed down by a $16 million VIP hold shortfall and a mass hold roughly 250bps below last year. The incremental costs from the full quarter Gourmet Pavilion and variable volume-related expenses also played a role.

As for the balance sheet, Wynn’s balance sheet is strong with total liquidity of $4.7 billion, most of that coming in the form of revolvers. Debt is structured at both the holdco and subsidiary levels. At the subsidiary level, the company’s Macau operations have two entities (Wynn Macau/Wynn Palace structures) that are currently carrying $5.79 billion of debt. At the holdco level, Wynn Resorts Finance (WRF), the company, carries $3.28 billion of debt. There’s also $877 million of debt against Las Vegas and another $598 million held in a JV.

While this structure may be confusing, Wynn does this to limit its exposure in the event of default and isolate its risk. With leverage at 4.4x company-wide (weighted average against all entities), the company has strong free cash flow generation that supports capex spending for ongoing projects. This quarter, the company spent $171 million on capex and another $26 million in dividends ($0.25 multiplied by 103 million shares outstanding).

Several Levers for EPS Uplift

Looking ahead, I think Wynn is well-positioned this year, given signs in Q4 that things are improving. Las Vegas is seeing casino volumes and RevPAR hold up well, Boston remains solid aside from weather-related dips, and Macau posted January volumes slightly above Q4 levels. This month, with Chinese New Year, Wynn should start to see an additional lift with the expanded Chairman's Club at Wynn Palace now approved and open, which management is describing as something that “will delight [their] best customers.” So while no Q1 guidance was provided, I do think these tailwinds set up the company well for next quarter.

But for the rest of 2026, I’d expect another good year. One of the reasons for this is that Macau’s GGR recovery has re-accelerated, with multiple houses now looking for high single- to double‑digit growth, driven disproportionately by premium mass. That directly benefits Wynn given its outsized exposure to premium customers and recent commentary about deliberately investing in VIP hosting and player development. As well, management highlighted that 2026 drivers are intact despite renovation headwinds, with group booking pace in Las Vegas “doing really, really well,” which lets them yield transient and casino segments harder on rate and mix. That aligns with my view that group/convention strength will offset the Encore Tower disruption via higher ADRs rather than chasing occupancy.

To put it in perspective, if Macau continues to grow in high single digits with premium outpacing base mass, I think Wynn can drive EBITDA via mix and rate even if China macro is only mediocre while Vegas fills the gap with higher‑rated group business that makes up for fewer Encore room nights.

As we’ve already dug into, Wynn is coming into 2026 with substantial liquidity versus its capex plan and no existential refinancing overhang, which gives them room to keep investing in guest products (Macau premium, Vegas rooms, Al Marjan) without sacrificing resilience. That balance sheet context makes it easier to underwrite a good year in 2026. With $750-850 million in capex for the development of Al Marjan Island, the company should start to see payback on this investment once it’s up and running next year.

Beyond 2026, I think there’s a multi-year story here with Al Marjan. Right now, it’s on track for a spring 2027 opening as a large‑scale integrated resort in the UAE, with the tower topped out around 70 floors and significant structural work already complete. The property will bring over 1,200 rooms plus nearly 300 high‑end suites, a large F&B and entertainment footprint, and the first true integrated resort experience in that market. Combined with premium‑heavy footprints in Las Vegas and Macau, I think that’ll give the company a portfolio leveraged to upper‑income consumers across the U.S., China, and the Gulf, all tied into wealth creation and outbound travel trends rather than just one macro cycle. On that point, it’s clear to me that management is long-term focused, and on the call they think about that a decade out, asking, “Where will we be in 2030 and 2032, and what will our business look like?” noting that they “feel very good about it” over the long haul.

Risks to Consider

With several casino properties that are tied to global gaming trends, it’s no secret that when times are tough, Wynn would get hurt in a downturn. If wealthy tourists stopped traveling and spending at Wynn properties, that would have a negative impact on the business. In a situation where revenues fall off and EBITDA gets cut in half, leverage would spike upwards. Mitigating this risk is the fact that a lot of the debt is held at the subsidiary level and the company enjoys a low cost of capital with no bonds trading with a spread more than 242bps above government debt. That indicates that the company should have little issue refinancing debt, at least the way things stand right now. Nevertheless, the business remains heavily exposed to Macau, where regulatory shifts, geopolitical tensions involving China, or renewed economic softness could materially impact premium and VIP volumes that have driven recent growth.

Second, the Wynn Al Marjan Island project in the UAE introduces a bit of risk in the sense that even with this being a greenfield development, it’s still a nascent gaming market, and delays beyond the Q1’27 opening or cost overruns could require a bigger equity commitment and delay future cash flows. Once this comes online, though, I do expect the company’s leverage to come down below 4x.

Wynn's Valuation is Justified Looking Out a Few Years

Wynn is rated favorably by the sellside with all ‘buy’ ratings and only 1 ‘hold’ rating. With an average price target of $142.79, analysts see about 24.5% upside from today’s levels, not including the company’s 0.88% dividend (source: Bloomberg). Speaking of the dividend, Wynn has a low payout ratio of 23%, paying 25 cents quarterly. After having cut its dividend in 2020 and then resuming it in 2023, there haven’t been any increases, but I’m okay with this given that the company is prioritizing safety and certainty of the dividend rather than chasing dividend growth, which we know can and has gotten it into trouble when cycles turn.

On valuation, Wynn looks optically expensive at 20.5x P/E and 9.2x EV/EBITDA. Both metrics are above peers, which trade at 16.6x P/E and 7.6x EV/EBITDA (source: Bloomberg). In my view, Wynn deserves its premium to peers for a few reasons. For one, Wynn’s entire model is built around the very top of the market (premium mass and VIP) rather than broad mid‑market exposure, which drives higher average customer worth and win per table/slot than competitors. As an example, Wynn properties have generated more gaming wins with fewer tables and machines than peers like MGM Resorts (MGM), which indicates that the assets and database are structurally more productive.

Another reason is the fact that Wynn’s future growth rates justify its higher TTM valuation multiples. When looking at analyst estimates for FY’26 and beyond, the company is expected to generate $8.12 in EPS in 2030, nearly double the $4.41 in EPS it’s generated in the last four quarters. So for a business that’s continuing to grow its EPS at a double-digit rate through the end of the decade, this valuation is justified.

Takeaway

All in all, I see 2026 as a year when Wynn grows into its premium positioning and capital program by absorbing known renovation and macro noise while setting up 2027–2030 as a period of accelerating free cash flow and better geographic balance, with Macau premiums, Las Vegas group demand, and Al Marjan’s ramp all pulling in the same direction. To me, this is one of the more underfollowed names in the market.

I think there’s a bit of a tug of war happening where traditional REIT and hospitality investors look at the 2026 P/E and balk but aren’t focused enough on what the true earnings power of this business will be if we just look three to five years out. With a strong balance sheet and a pipeline of opportunities to invest in and diversify growth internationally, I’m long Wynn Resorts.

data.title: PEJ: Modest Upside With Meaningful Constraints
data.description:
The Invesco Leisure and Entertainment ETF (PEJ) has had somewhat of a lackluster past, with total returns since inception failing to historically match the broader market as a whole.

With the fund being concentrated in highly cyclical sectors, one would expect to be rewarded for an increased level of risk-taking. Additionally, given certain fund dynamics [namely, a high portfolio turnover and quarterly rebalancing], PEJ does not seem well-suited for longer-term investors.

My investment thesis is the following: Given what I perceive to be structural flaws (quarterly rebalancing leading to 75% portfolio turnover), in addition to historical underperformance, valuation, and thematic exposure suggest modest forward returns. For these reasons, I rate PEJ a ‘Hold.’

If we were to see evidence of durable earnings acceleration among airlines, hotels, and entertainment stocks, this could serve as a justification to reconsider my position. A narrowing of PEJ’s valuation discount relative to the S&P 500 SPY, driven by strong earnings growth, would likewise call for a more bullish stance.

On the bearish side, if we were to see demand for leisure and entertainment decrease, driven by lower levels of consumer spending, this could potentially serve as a trigger to go from a Hold position to a Sell.

Fund History

The Invesco Leisure and Entertainment ETF has an inception dating back to June 2005, giving us more than 20 years of management and performance to consider. According to the fund’s website, its objective is the following:

“[The fund] is based on the Dynamic Leisure & Entertainment Intellidex℠ Index [and] will normally invest at least 90% of its total assets in common stocks that comprise the Index. The Index is designed to provide capital appreciation by thoroughly evaluating companies based on a variety of investment merit criteria.”

Furthermore, Invesco offers a vast lineup of different ETFs with the goal of providing investors with diverse and customized portfolios to meet individuals’ investment objectives.

Index

As mentioned, the fund tracks the Dynamic Leisure & Entertainment Intellidex℠ Index. This index exists specifically for the Invesco fund.

The index is comprised of U.S. leisure and entertainment companies and is designed to provide capital appreciation by selecting companies based on a variety of investment criteria. This criterion includes fundamental growth, stock valuation, investment timeliness, and risk factors.

Constituents include 30 US common stocks of leisure and entertainment companies. A screening mechanism is implemented to include companies that are primarily engaged in the design, production, or distribution of goods or services in the leisure and entertainment space. The Fund and the Index are rebalanced and reconstituted quarterly in February, May, August, and November.

Holdings and Performance

Top Holdings

The fund maintains holdings of approximately 30 companies, with the top ten holdings accounting for greater than 48% of all holdings. Given the small number of holdings and tilt towards the top ten, the portfolio is highly concentrated.

While the fund aims to provide exposure to entertainment and leisure companies, the definition of this is rather broad. Investors will notice companies such as Sysco, DoorDash, and Uber all appear in the top ten, making up 5.72%, 4.07%, and 4.05% of the portfolio. While arguments can be made for these companies being leisure-adjacent, it is hard to consider them pure leisure and entertainment plays.

Sector Allocation

As for the sector breakdown, the fund is divided among consumer cyclical, communication, industrials, and consumer defensive. At a more granular level, hotels, restaurants & leisure make up 41.12% of all holdings, followed by entertainment (25.90%) and passenger airlines (10.69%).

While current holdings show a concentration in hotels, restaurants, and leisure, this is likely to change during the next rebalance. As previously mentioned, the Index is rebalanced and reconstituted quarterly in February, May, August, and November. Based on the last semi-annual report from October 31, 2025, the portfolio turnover rate was 75%.

With such a high portfolio churn and such frequent rebalancing, investors should approach PEJ with caution. With positions frequently changing, the fund may prove to be an ineffective way for long-term investors to gain exposure to the leisure and entertainment space. The image below shows sector allocations during the last semi-annual and annual reports.

Forward Outlook

Unlike broad market ETFs that have significantly benefited from mega-cap technology, PEJ is much more sensitive to underlying economic conditions. Both consumer confidence and discretionary spending trends are connected to the overall performance of the fund.

With the US economy continuing to expand at a moderate pace, we expect factors such as real wage growth to support areas such as travel, dining, and entertainment. With that said, we expect PEJ to benefit from steady revenue growth and margin stabilization.

Valuation also plays a role in the forward outlook. With PEJ’s valuation multiples currently sitting at meaningful discounts compared to the S&P 500, PEJ does not need aggressive multiple expansion to provide moderate earnings growth.

All in all, PEJ’s current positioning is neither overly positive nor negative. Instead, the fund appears positioned for moderate forward returns, largely based on US economic performance and consumer spending activity.

Historical Performance

Since the inception of the fund in 2005, PEJ has consistently underperformed compared to the S&P 500 (SPY). During this time, the S&P has returned nearly 970%, while PEJ has returned a mere 371% in comparison. This trend continues today, with one-year, five-year, and 10-year returns all muted when compared to the broader market.

Valuation

On a valuation basis, PEJ can generally be considered cheaper when compared to the S&P 500. In terms of price-to-earnings, price-to-book, price-to-sales, and price-to-cash flow, the fund compares favorably to the broad market.

While PEJ has historically underperformed, it has done so in a bull market largely driven by mega-cap tech companies. With materially lower valuation multiples, one might assume that forward returns may not require overly bold assumptions.

Currently, PEJ trades at an approximately 29% discount to the S&P 500 on a trailing earnings basis (19.87x compared to 27.86x). Historically, leisure and travel equities tend to trade at a modest discount to the broader market given their cyclicality of earnings. Should the discount between PEJ and SPY compress meaningfully below this amount without a corresponding rise in earnings, valuation support would become less compelling.

Fund-Specific Factors

PEJ has a Seeking Alpha Momentum grade of D+. The fund has struggled to gain momentum for some time now, with one-month, six-month, and one-year total returns all underperforming (-3.35%, 2.35%, 5.63%). While three-year total returns were slightly higher than the median of all other ETFs (46.44% compared to 41.44%), the fund has generally underperformed the median historically. While underperformance compared to the broader market has been the norm for PEJ, the fund would stand to benefit from a catalyst or narrative shift.

PEJ also receives a Seeking Alpha Dividend grade of D+. With a dividend yield of 0.24% and its last dividend payment of $0.07 paid on December 26, 2025, the fund ranks significantly below all other ETFs (median dividend for all other ETFs equals 2.64%). While PEJ provides investors exposure to select leisure and entertainment companies, income-specific investors would be advised to look for better-yielding opportunities elsewhere.

In terms of the fund’s expense ratio of 0.57%, it is not significantly higher than the median of all other ETFs (with an expense ratio of 0.50%). However, when compared to SPY with an expense ratio of 0.09%, PEJ’s expense ratio can be considered high. One would expect some outperformance compared to the S&P 500, given the additional cost to investors.

Risks

In terms of potential risks that could affect my underlying ‘Hold’ thesis, there are two main scenarios to consider.

In terms of the price of PEJ rising significantly higher, I see continued consumer resilience, market rotation into value/cyclicals, and a narrowing of the valuation discount compared to SPY as all being potentially problematic to my ‘Hold’ thesis.

As for the price of PEJ moving lower, possibilities such as an economic slowdown and continuation of tech dominance also negatively affect my ‘Hold’ rating.

Various other factors should also present cause for concern among investors. Namely, the fund’s quarterly rebalancing schedule and 75% turnover rate indicate that investors buying the fund today will likely own a very different basket of holdings in a year from now. This may be problematic for more passive, longer-term investors.

Additionally, historical underperformance, marginal dividend payment, and the highly cyclical nature of the fund are also areas of concern that investors must consider before investing in PEJ.

Conclusion

In my opinion, the fund is faced with both structural flaws and historical underperformance. However, valuation and thematic exposure still suggest modest forward returns largely driven by modest economic gains and valuation support.

While PEJ does provide investors an easy way to gain exposure to the leisure and entertainment space, it is my opinion that, given current conditions, the fund is neither compelling enough to Buy nor Sell. For these reasons, I rate PEJ a ‘Hold.’

Investors should continue to monitor the fund’s price-to-trailing earnings in comparison with the S&P 500, in addition to the regular quarterly rebalancing, resulting in high portfolio churn. Should the discount between PEJ and SPY compress meaningfully below current levels (~29%) without a corresponding rise in earnings, valuation support would become less compelling.

data.title: Thrivent Small Cap Stock Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Executive Summary

  • The Fund underperformed the Russell 2000 Index over the last quarter mainly due to allocation effects and underperformed the past year mainly due to security selection. The largest detractors to the past quarter were in Health Care, and largest detractors to the last year were in Health Care and Industrials.
  • New positions were initiated in multiple sectors, taking advantage of the volatility in stock prices.
  • The focus remains on finding quality companies that are attractively priced with long runways for growth in sales, earnings, and free cash flow.

Performance factors

During the past quarter, the Fund underperformed mainly due to allocation effects. Negative allocation was led by the Health Care sector, while Materials was the largest positive contributor. The Portfolio's underweight positioning in Pharmaceuticals & Biotechnology industries detracted from returns, as both segments delivered strong performance during the quarter. We continue to evaluate opportunities in these areas for investments that align with our long-term investment criteria.

During the quarter we initiated a position in Texas Roadhouse (TXRH), a casual dining restaurant company that operates a chain of American steakhouses. The recent escalation in beef costs has compressed the company's margins and pressured the share price. Given the inherent cyclicality of beef prices and the company's strong value proposition, which underpins its pricing power, we anticipate a meaningful improvement in operating performance over the intermediate term. Our expectation of improved operating outcomes coupled with a long runway for continued growth in cash flows allowed us to add a quality company to the portfolio at an attractive price.

Over the past 12-months, the Fund underperformed largely due to negative security selection. Negative security selection was led by the Health Care sector and in particular, Twist Biosciences (TWST) and ADMA Biologics (ADMA). Twist Biosciences was adversely affected by the reduction in the National Institutes of Health funding earlier this year. We nonetheless view the company as a continuing share gainer within the synthetic DNA ecosystem.

Sector allocation for the past year was negative in several sectors, with the largest contributor being the underweight position in Health Care.

Portfolio outlook

With the current level of business uncertainty and the Federal Funds rate speculation, it is unclear by how much and for how long the economy will be impacted. However, investor sentiment about the economy fluctuates much more rapidly and with greater amplitude than actual changes in activity. These swings in economic sentiment often result in sharp movements in stock prices, which can present opportunities. As a result, positions were added to or initiated in multiple sectors, while other positions were reduced or sold as investment thesis played out or proved no longer valid. Our focus remains on finding solid companies that are attractively priced with long runways ahead of them for growth in sales, earnings, and free cash flow.

data.title: Harbor Dividend Growth Leaders ETF Q4 Contributors & Detractors
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Harbor Dividend Growth Leaders ETF Q4 2025 Commentary.

During the fourth quarter, the Harbor Dividend Growth Leaders ETF (GDIV) ("ETF") returned 3.47% ('NAV'), outperforming the style benchmark, the NASDAQ US Dividend Achievers Select Total Return Index ("Index"), which returned 1.53%, and its primary benchmark, the S&P 500 Index, which returned 2.66%.

From a sector perspective, relative strength in Consumer Discretionary and Information Technology offset relative weakness within Communication Services and Financials.

The relative outperformance of the ETF, compared to the Index, was primarily driven by positive stock selection, but the ETF also benefited from a common factor tailwind during the quarter. From a factor perspective, the ETF's overweight exposures to earnings variation — a proxy for more cyclically oriented stocks — was a tailwind to performance, offsetting a headwind from being underweight growth relative to the Index.

Dividend growth strategies recovered ground in the fourth quarter, capping a year marked by stark contrasts between speculative rallies and quality-led resilience. Throughout much of 2025, markets were dominated by narrow leadership and risk-seeking behavior, particularly in the first half of the year, when momentum-driven trades and high-beta stocks outpaced fundamentals. Dividend growth — by design, a quality and defensively oriented strategy — lagged during these periods as investors' focus skewed toward thematic exposure and near-term upside.

However, the backdrop began to shift meaningfully in the second half of the year. The Fed's pivot toward rate cuts, alongside easing inflation, a stable consumer, and moderating labor trends, helped improve financial conditions without reigniting excess. In the fourth quarter, market breadth broadened materially: Small- and mid-cap stocks outperformed mega caps, cyclicals gained traction, and sector leadership rotated toward Financials, Industrials, and Health Care — areas more aligned with dividend growth portfolios.

By year-end, the market appeared to be transitioning from a liquidity-fueled, speculative phase toward one increasingly driven by earnings quality and valuation discipline. While full-year returns favored early-cycle risk taking, the late-year pivot reinforced the long-term appeal of companies with durable cash flows, strong balance sheets, and a demonstrated commitment to dividend growth. As investor positioning normalizes and volatility remains episodic, we believe the foundation is in place for quality dividend strategies to outperform in the next phase of the cycle.

Contributors & Detractors

The top five contributors to relative performance during the quarter were: Mueller Industries, Cardinal Health (CAH), Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group (SMFG), Expedia, and Tapestry (TPR). (TPR)

Mueller Industries, a manufacturer of materials and components used in industrial applications, outperformed during the quarter, as the company reported strong quarterly results with net income and earnings-per-share ("EPS") growth ahead of Wall St. expectations. We continue to believe that the company's durable end-market positioning in plumbing, HVAC/R, and industrial components should support growth through mixed cycles. We did not make any adjustments to our position during the quarter.

Cardinal Health, a distributor of medical and lab supplies, outperformed on the back of a strong earnings report, driven by earnings growth ahead of investor expectations and raised fiscal year 2026 guidance. We maintain conviction in the thesis but reduced our position on strength during the quarter.

Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group, a dominant Japanese bank, outperformed during the quarter as the company reported strong second-quarter earnings results, with EPS significantly ahead of Wall St. expectations. We did not make any adjustments to our position during the quarter.

Expedia, an online travel company, outperformed during the quarter following strong third-quarter earnings, with EPS and sales ahead of expectations. We did not make any adjustments to our position during the quarter.

Tapestry, a leading luxury accessories and lifestyle company, was a key contributor within the Consumer Discretionary sector. Shares advanced on continued strength from the Coach brand, whose ongoing brand elevation and successful innovation strategy continued to resonate with consumers, supporting strong top-line growth and margin leverage. Increased desirability enabled meaningful price increases without promotional pressure, while the brand's ability to attract and retain younger customers reinforced potential long-term relevance. We did not make any adjustments to our position during the quarter.

The top five detractors from relative performance during the quarter were: Arthur J. Gallagher & Co, Meta Platforms, International Paper, Williams Companies, and Cintas (CTAS).

Arthur J. Gallagher & Co, an insurance brokerage and risk management service provider, underperformed following a disappointing earnings report stating that the company missed its earnings target, driven by slower-than-anticipated organic growth. While we continue to like the long-term story, we decided to step aside from the name for the time being, as a source of funds for other names that we believe have a better risk/reward profile.

Meta Platforms, a social media platform, underperformed during the quarter as investor concern around lack of visibility into 2026 expense growth and return on investment from AI CapEx spending weighed on sentiment. While we continue to believe that the company is well positioned to emerge as a leader in AI with a long runway for revenue growth, we decided to step aside from the name for the time being, as a source of funds for other names that we believe have a better risk/reward profile.

International Paper, a global producer of fiber-based packaging and pulp, underperformed over the period following a disappointing earnings report and lowered guidance for 2026 and 2027, driven by macro headwinds from consumer and industrial weakness. Despite operational improvements and progress in the company's turnaround story, we decided to step aside as the turnaround unfolds and monitor for clearer evidence that operational improvements are translating into sustainable performance.

Williams Companies, an energy infrastructure company specializing in natural gas and petroleum, underperformed after reporting EPS slightly below consensus expectations. Despite the recent underperformance, we maintain conviction in the potential role of midstream natural gas in a world involving power infrastructure build-out driven by AI demands. We added to our position during the quarter.

Cintas, a provider of corporate uniforms, apparel, and supplies, underperformed alongside broader defensive services, despite reporting positive earnings results during the quarter. In our view, fundamentals remain intact with an attractive business model, with steady free-cash-flow generation and a path to margin expansion. We did not make any adjustments to our position during the quarter.

Buy and Sells

Looking forward, consensus points to more balanced EPS growth across market segments in 2026, supporting a healthier and less concentrated earnings backdrop. Cyclicals are trading at a meaningful discount and showing accelerating profit growth, creating attractive opportunities where fundamentals are improving faster than prices.

Along these lines, we have become more constructive on the Energy sector, given oil prices, valuations, and sentiment, and added exposure to the sector during the quarter. For example, we purchased Diamondback Energy (FANG), an independent oil and gas company concentrating on the acquisition, development, and production of unconventional onshore oil and natural gas reserves in the Permian Basin.

Within Financials, we added exposure to banks with the purchase of Citigroup (C) and sold our position in insurance brokerage and risk management service provider, Arthur J. Gallagher, as a source of funds.

We also added biopharma exposure within Health Care, where we added a number of new positions during the quarter, including global pharmaceutical companies Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) and AbbVie (ABBV). Large pharmaceutical companies have faced a multiyear overhang from most-favored-nations pricing that has resulted in the opportunity to buy good growth stories at attractive relative valuations, in our opinion.

In contrast, we reduced exposure to Real Estate during the quarter and sold our positions in Welltower (WELL), a health care REIT, and STAG Industrial (STAG), an industrial REIT, as sources of funds for other names that we believe have better risk/reward profiles.

This means that the Predecessor Fund's performance and financial history will be used by the Fund going forward from the date of reorganization.

In the reorganization, former shareholders of the Predecessor Fund received shares of the Fund. Accordingly, the performance of the Fund for periods prior to the reorganization is the performance of the Predecessor Fund and the performance shown for periods prior to July 26, 2013 is the performance of a private investment vehicle that predated the Predecessor Fund. The private investment vehicle was managed by the Subadvisor using investment policies, objectives and guidelines that were in all material respects equivalent to the management of the Fund and Predecessor Fund. However, the private investment vehicle was not a registered investment company and so it was not subject to the same investment and tax restrictions as the Fund and Predecessor Fund. If it had been, its performance may have been lower. The performance of the Predecessor Fund has not been restated to reflect the annual operating expenses of the Fund, which are lower than those of the Predecessor Fund. Because the Fund has different fees and expenses than the Predecessor Fund, the Fund would also have had different performance results.

Important Information

Active Currency Contribution is the Currency Contribution of the portfolio minus the Currency Contribution of the benchmark.

Allocation Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return that is attributable to taking different group bets from the benchmark. (If either the portfolio or the benchmark has no position in a given group, allocation effect is the lone effect.) A group's allocation effect equals the average percent capitalization of the portfolio's group minus the average percent cap of the benchmark's group times the total return of the benchmark group minus the total return of the benchmark.

Average Weight is the dollar value (price times the shares held) of the security or group, divided by the total dollar value of the entire portfolio displayed as a percentage. It is calculated as the simple arithmetic average of daily values.

Contribution to Return is the contribution of a security or group to the overall portfolio return. It is calculated as the security weight multiplied by the daily security return linked daily across the reporting period.

Currency Contribution is Total Return in USD subtracting out the Local Returns.

Local Returns are the Total Return of the portfolio or benchmark using the local currency.

Selection Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return attributable to choosing different securities within groups from the benchmark. A group's security selection effect equals the average weight of the benchmark's group times the total return of the portfolio's group minus the total return of the benchmark's group.

Total Effect is the sum of Allocation Effect and Selection Effect. The total effect represents the opportunity cost of what was done in a group relative to the overall portfolio. It is not just the difference between percent contribution in the portfolio and benchmark. At the overall portfolio level, the two numbers are equal. At the group level, they can be different.

Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

Definitions

Free cash flow represents the cash a company can generate after accounting for capital expenditures needed to maintain or maximize its asset base.

Beta is a measure of systematic risk, or the sensitivity of a fund to movements in the benchmark. A beta of 1 implies that the expected movement of a fund's return would match that of the benchmark used to measure beta.

Earnings Per Share ('EPS') is the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share.

Capital expenditure (CapEx) refers to money spent on major, long-term business assets such as property, equipment, or technology.

Sector Overweights and Underweights

Communication Services represented the ETF's largest overweight relative to the Index entering 2025. We subsequently sold both positions that we held entering 2025 and do not have any exposure to Communications Services as of year-end 2025. This is in line with the Index, which also does not have any exposure to that sector as of year-end.

The Consumer Staples sector represented the ETF's largest underweight entering 2025 and continues to be a large underweight as of year-end. Active Currency Contribution is the Currency Contribution of the portfolio minus the Currency Contribution of the benchmark.

Allocation Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return that is attributable to taking different group bets from the benchmark. (If either the portfolio or the benchmark has no position in a given group, allocation effect is the lone effect.) A group's allocation effect equals the average percent capitalization of the portfolio's group minus the average percent cap of the benchmark's group times the total return of the benchmark group minus the total return of the benchmark.

Average Weight is the dollar value (price times the shares held) of the security or group, divided by the total dollar value of the entire portfolio displayed as a percentage. It is calculated as the simple arithmetic average of daily values.

Contribution to Return is the contribution of a security or group to the overall portfolio return. It is calculated as the security weight multiplied by the daily security return linked daily across the reporting period.

Currency Contribution is Total Return in USD subtracting out the Local Returns.

Local Returns are the Total Return of the portfolio or benchmark using the local currency.

Selection Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return attributable to choosing different securities within groups from the benchmark. A group's security selection effect equals the average weight of the benchmark's group times the total return of the portfolio's group minus the total return of the benchmark's group.

Total Effect is the sum of Allocation Effect and Selection Effect. The total effect represents the opportunity cost of what was done in a group relative to the overall portfolio. It is not just the difference between percent contribution in the portfolio and benchmark. At the overall portfolio level, the two numbers are equal. At the group level, they can be different.

Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

Definitions

Free cash flow represents the cash a company can generate after accounting for capital expenditures needed to maintain or maximize its asset base.

Beta is a measure of systematic risk, or the sensitivity of a fund to movements in the benchmark. A beta of 1 implies that the expected movement of a fund's return would match that of the benchmark used to measure beta.

Earnings Per Share ('EPS') is the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share.

Capital expenditure (CapEx) refers to money spent on major, long-term business assets such as property, equipment, or technology.

data.title: Can Emerging Markets ETFs Enhance Your Technology Exposure?
data.description:
Emerging Markets technology is becoming a critical driver of global innovation, offering investors exposure to AI, semiconductors, and digital infrastructure while trading at lower price-to-earnings multiples than US technology.

US equity valuations remain elevated, and concentration risk is now at a decade-high, driven by a narrow group of mega-cap technology stocks. While we believe US innovation leadership remains strong, some forward-looking portfolios may consider diversification into areas where fundamentals have shown improvement and valuations have been lower relative to US technology, based on selected metrics, such as emerging markets technology.*

We believe the emerging markets (EM) technology sector represents one of the most compelling allocation opportunities right now. Here is why:

A Potentially Weaker US Dollar Supports Emerging Markets Technology Firms.

A softer US dollar has historically been supportive of emerging markets technology and broader EM assets, as local currency cash flows translate into more attractive returns for dollar‑based investors. When the dollar is weaker or more range‑bound, financial conditions in many EM economies tend to improve, lowering funding pressures and supporting equity market performance. Policy signals from the current US administration, including its focus on domestic manufacturing and reshoring, imply a tolerance—if not a preference—for a more competitive dollar to support exports, which indirectly enhances the backdrop for emerging markets technology companies linked to global trade and supply chains.

AI Capex Is Powering Asian Emerging Markets Supply Chains.

While artificial intelligence (AI) demand is often discussed through the lens of US mega‑caps, much of the physical build‑out of AI systems actually happens in Asia, led by emerging markets technology companies in semiconductors, memory, and advanced packaging. EM technology firms, including Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSMC) and SK Hynix, play important roles in global AI hardware and memory supply chains and are exposed to ongoing AI capital expenditure ("AI Capex") across data centers, semiconductors, and advanced computing. American companies contract out the manufacturing of their chips to these companies.

Emerging Markets Technology Could Be at an Early Stage of A Potential New Profit Growth Cycle.

Many EM technology companies recently executed inventory corrections and cost rationalization that have been viewed positively by analysts.1 Today, we are seeing improving profitability expectations, operating leverage, and balance-sheet metrics, alongside lower price-to-earnings multiples than those of US technology peers.

Analysts’ estimates for long-term earnings per share (EPS) growth have also picked up significantly since June, placing them on par with the average estimates for US technology companies.

Due to high concentration in US equities,2 stretched valuations, and AI investment broadening globally,3 we believe EM technology represents a differentiated way to participate in long-term growth themes as part of a broader diversification approach.*

How to invest in Emerging Markets technology

The KraneShares Emerging Markets Consumer Technology ETF (KEMQ) provides targeted exposure to technology and internet companies across Asia and other emerging markets, positioning investors for this next phase of the global tech cycle. The KraneShares SSE STAR Market 50 Index ETF (KSTR) is a potential option for investors to dial-up their exposure to China’s technology sector in addition to broader emerging markets. KSTR provides exposure to the 50 largest companies listed on China’s STAR Market, which is dedicated to companies innovating in information technology, biomedicine, and new energy.

Key holdings in KEMQ include:

SK Hynix (HXSC.F)(4.3% of Net Assets as of 1/28/2026)

SK Hynix is a South Korean semiconductor manufacturer that specializes in memory chips such as DRAM and NAND flash, as well as related products like SSDs and image sensors. DRAM (dynamic random‑access memory) is the high‑speed, short‑term working memory that computers and servers use to store data they are actively processing, which must be refreshed constantly to retain information. NAND flash is a non-volatile type of memory that keeps data even when power is turned off and is widely used in solid‑state drives and other storage devices. SK Hynix is one of the world’s largest memory chip makers (commonly ranked second after Samsung) and a key supplier to major global technology companies and data centers, and in recent years it has drawn particular attention for its advanced AI memory, including high‑bandwidth memory (HBM) used in cutting‑edge AI accelerators.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (TSMC) (TSM)(3.76% of Net Assets as of 1/28/2026)

TSMC is a Taiwanese semiconductor company that operates as the world’s leading dedicated contract chip manufacturer, or “pure‑play” foundry. It fabricates advanced logic chips for major global customers, including Apple, Nvidia, AMD, and Qualcomm, powering products from smartphones to cloud data centers and AI supercomputers. TSMC is notable both for its dominant share of the global foundry market and for its cutting-edge process technologies used in the most advanced AI and high-performance computing chips.

Alibaba (BABA)(3.48% of Net Assets as of 1/28/2026)

Alibaba is a China-based technology platform covering E-Commerce, AI cloud services, logistics, media, and local services. According to publicly reported data, Alibaba’s Qwen large language model (LLM) is one of the most used models in China and is highly sought after by global corporations.4

Key holdings in KSTR include:

Cambricon Technologies (10.6% of Net Assets as of 1/28/2026)

A China-based AI chip designer. Cambricon’s processors are increasingly used in local AI servers as China-based companies expand domestic AI computing infrastructure.

Hygon Information (9.9% of Net Assets as of 1/28/2026)

Hygon is a key x86 CPU provider serving China’s data center and AI server markets. Hygon helps power the core computing engines that run cloud and AI workloads. An x86 CPU is a general‑purpose processor based on the widely used x86 instruction set architecture, originally developed for personal computers and now standard in most servers. Because so much existing software, operating systems, and cloud infrastructure is built around x86, these CPUs are the workhorses of modern data centers. Hygon's processors are used in domestic cloud and AI infrastructure as enterprises and hyperscalers expand domestic compute capacity.

Montage Technology (MNTCF) (6.4% of Net Assets as of 1/28/2026)

A critical supplier of memory interface and connectivity chips used in AI servers, Montage plays a behind‑the‑scenes but essential role in enabling high‑performance AI workloads. DDR5, the latest generation of double data rate (DDR) memory, allows data to be transferred much faster and more efficiently between processors and memory than prior standards, which is vital for training and running large AI models. By providing DDR5‑compatible memory interface chips and data‑center connectivity solutions, Montage helps ensure that AI servers can move and process the massive volumes of data required for modern artificial intelligence.

*Diversification does not ensure a profit or guarantee against a loss.

Citations:

  1. Medeiros, Gustavo. “A new bull market cycle in Emerging Market equities,” Ashmore. September 2024. Retrieved on 12/31/2025.
  2. Based on the weight of the top ten constituents in the S&P 500 Index as a percentage of the total index. Data from Bloomberg as of 12/31/2025.
  3. “Economy.” The 2025 AI Index Report, Stanford HAI. December 31, 2025.
  4. Jiang, Ben. "Alibaba's Qwen family hits 700 million downloads to lead global open-source AI adoption," South China Morning Post. January 12, 2026.

Definitions:

Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio: A stock valuation metric that shows how much investors are willing to pay for each dollar of a company's earnings, calculated by dividing the current share price by the earnings per share. It helps assess if a stock is overvalued (high P/E) or undervalued (low P/E) compared to its peers or its own history, often indicating growth expectations (higher P/E) or perceived risk (lower P/E).

Nasdaq 100 Index: The Nasdaq-100 Index tracks 100 of the largest non-financial companies listed on the NASDAQ stock market, including major tech, retail, and healthcare firms, and was launched on January 31, 1985, as a benchmark for innovation-driven growth, not including financial firms.

Earnings Per Share (EPS): A company's total profit over a 12-month period divided by the number of shares outstanding.

Est. 3-5 Year EPS Growth: Annualized growth expected for a company's earnings per share over at least a three-year period. For the purposes of this communication, publicly available estimates as far into the future as five but no less than three years are used for the constituents of an index or holdings of a fund to generate a harmonized average.

data.title: Sandisk: The Bust Cycle Could Be Coming Sooner Than Expected
data.description:
Sandisk (SNDK) is well known to many consumers for its memory cards used in electronic devices. However, the company is increasingly focused on providing storage solutions for AI data centers. The boom in AI capex has greatly benefited this company, causing the share price to surge. I believe the easy money has been made in this stock, and shareholders could be disappointed with expectations now so high. After a huge surge in the share price, a lot of good news is already priced into this stock. Furthermore, even a hint of a slowdown in AI capex spend could cause a major correction in this stock. Considering this, let's examine why this stock has surged, creating fortunes for many shareholders, and why significant downside risks may emerge going forward.

The Chart

Take a look at the chart below, and you might think you are looking at Nvidia (NVDA), which some believe is the "poster child" for the AI trade. However, Nvidia shares have been stuck in a trading range for months. In contrast, as the chart below shows, Sandisk shares have surged. This stock traded for less than $50 per share as recently as August, but it now trades for about $625 per share. This stock is overbought and well above the 50-day moving average, which is around $382. The current share price is nearly 4 times the 200-day moving average, which is around $164.

Why Fortunes Have Been Made

The hyperscalers have launched an unprecedented "arms race" to develop AI infrastructure, creating a boom for memory and storage companies like Sandisk. The memory and storage industry was unprepared for the AI capex spending surge, and this has led to huge demand and expanding profit margins as companies like Sandisk take advantage of the market. Sandisk has reported big Q2FY26 revenue increases across multiple segments of its business, which include: Consumer with a 39% quarter-over-quarter increase, or "QoQ", Edge with a 21% QoQ increase, and a whopping 64% QoQ increase for data center.

In 2024, Sandisk reported adjusted earnings of $2.14 per share. In 2025, the company's adjusted earnings surged to $22.40 per share. As shown below, the company is guiding for $12 to $14 in earnings for Q3. All of this shows how this stock surge has been driven by a surge in earnings. However, there is a real risk that this trend is not sustainable and that earnings will begin to decline by 2028, if not sooner. I believe that the risk for investors is that this stock will likely decline well before earnings begin to decline, and that is why I would cash in on the unprecedented gains this stock has seen.

Earnings Estimates And The Balance Sheet

As provided by Seeking Alpha, the earnings estimates below show that earnings are expected to surge from just under $40 per share in 2026 to over $80 per share in 2027. However, earnings are expected to start declining again in 2028, dropping nearly 10%. Stocks that have earnings declines typically trade at very low PE multiples. While the PE multiple is currently near 16, it is expected to drop to just below 9 times the 2028 estimates. The problem is that earnings could continue to decline significantly beyond 2028, as the storage industry potentially faces a combination of reduced demand and increased market supply, which could punish profit margins. Historically, these boom-and-bust cycles have occurred in the past. During each boom cycle, some have said this time is different, but I am skeptical of this view.
